text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic cholecystectomy using four standard ports, grasping the gallbladder at its fundus and retracting it upwards.
The surgeon holds the gallbladder at Hartman's pouch and uses a hook diathermy to dissect the posterior peritoneum, performing careful dissection in the calyx region.

The surgeon uses hook diathermy to divide both the posterior and anterior peritoneal attachments of the gallbladder from the liver bed, ensuring the dissection remains above the rubeus sulcus to prevent injury to the common bile duct, right sectoral ducts, and hepatic duct.

The surgeon carefully applies the hook diathermy in a direction towards the common bile duct (CBD) while holding it steady, ensuring precise dissection of the cystic duct. The gallbladder is further dissected free from the liver bed.
The surgeon applies two clips below and one clip above the cystic duct, then divides the duct using cold scissors. The dissection continues to skeletonize the cystic artery, revealing its structure.
The cystic artery is fully dissected and visible. It is clipped and divided using hook diathermy due to its thin structure.
The surgeon uses hook diathermy to dissect the gallbladder from the liver bed, completing the separation of the gallbladder in a straightforward case.

one should not get carried away during this step because a slight lack of attentiveness may cause a hole in the gallbladder causing spillage of bile and stones thereby making it a messy surgery And it also increases the time as well as the chances of infection for the patient. Always when one sees a simple gallbladder, it means that one has to be very cautious.
The surgeon completes the removal of the gallbladder from the liver bed, ensuring hemostasis as needed. The gallbladder specimen is then retrieved through the epigastric port.
